lessons.what_went_right: Anti-anchoring protocol held; research was multi-source.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Swung Feenstra 22pts on a single primary poll + unsanctioned straw polls.
lessons.lesson: Never move a forecast materially on one noisy data point (esp. a single poll); weight by reliability and require corroboration. Don't recommend fading a liquid market on a low-confidence estimate.

lessons.what_went_right: Identified the genuine bimodality and the Warsh wildcard; 0.42 was a defensible non-extreme number.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Let a vivid 'division' narrative pull the estimate below a liquid market pricing a strong base rate of 0 dissents, without a specific documented reason for the divergence.
lessons.lesson: When diverging below a liquid market that is pricing a strong institutional base rate, require a specific, verifiable reason; narrative of 'division' is not evidence of a formally recorded dissent. Anchor on the base rate.
lessons.judge_verdict: Forecaster (0.42) diverged below a liquid market (0.635) that correctly priced the strong institutional base rate of zero formal FOMC dissents. A 'most-divided-since-1992' narrative was allowed to override that base rate; committee division rhetoric != a formally recorded dissent. No position taken, so a calibration loss only. Defender right that 0.42 is non-extreme and one resolution is noisy, but the directional lean against the base rate was the real error.
lessons.disagreement: Critic called the divergence unjustified (process flaw); Defender framed it as reasonable disagreement plus single-point noise. The gap: was the sub-market lean a process error or variance?

lessons.what_went_right: Correctly saw transit was the key variable; market itself was near coin-flip, so the call was genuinely contested.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Anchored to a hoped-for recovery instead of the prevailing crisis-suppressed regime, and took a position despite only a coin-flip-level divergence from a liquid market built on an unverified assumption.
lessons.lesson: During an active supply-disruption crisis, anchor flow estimates to the SUPPRESSED regime, not an assumed recovery, until data confirms normalization; and do not enter a position when divergence from a liquid market is small and rests on an unverified assumption.
lessons.judge_verdict: Forecaster (0.61) assumed a transit RECOVERY toward ~35/week, but the resolution week (Jun 8-14) sat entirely inside the active blockade with traffic collapsed (~5/day, as confirmed by the current Hormuz-norm read). The ensemble baked in a normalization that had not occurred. Critically, a YES position was ENTERED on a near-coin-flip divergence (0.61 vs market 0.495) resting on that unverified recovery assumption -> realized loss. This is the overconfident-entry-on-thin-edge / stale-regime failure.
lessons.disagreement: Critic: divergence unjustified, single ensemble, no CI. Defender: directionally defensible, ceasefire only at window-end, count noise near the 25 line. The gap: was entering the position justified given the thin edge?

lessons.what_went_right: Uranium was topically central to the Jun-19 US-Iran deal; Trump using synonyms all week during peak nuclear coverage is a genuinely low-probability linguistic outcome.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Substituted topical salience for the verbatim-quote resolution rule; asserted 'already said it at the G7 Jun 18' without confirming it against the resolution source; took a 0.99 forecast (80pt fade) against a liquid market at 18% that was carrying real information.
lessons.lesson: For KXTRUMPSAY-style verbatim-utterance markets, resolution is a literal tracked quote from the resolution source, NOT topical salience — never forecast above the market without a CONFIRMED verbatim quote, and treat any 80pt fade against a liquid market as model error. This is exactly the case the new hard_gap_ceiling now hard-blocks regardless of confidence.
lessons.judge_verdict: MODEL ERROR, avoidable (not bad luck). Worst-possible Brier 0.98 vs market 0.03.
lessons.disagreement: Critic (blind) flagged market_divergence_justified as the biggest miss; defender conceded the synonym-avoidance was hard to foresee but the unverified 'already said it' premise made the 0.99 indefensible — judge sided with the critic.

lessons.what_went_right: Established a base rate and reasoned the divergence.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Faded a liquid market on thin (<3 independent) sourcing; overconfident point estimate.
lessons.lesson: On down-ballot foreign nominations, do not diverge from a liquid market without >=3 independent primary sources — now enforced by the Qwen-browser retrieval (>5 disparate).
lessons.judge_verdict: Critic+defender agree: base rate ok, market-divergence reasoned, but <3 independent sources and no explicit CI. Resolved NO; market (0.018) beat mine (0.176).

lessons.what_went_right: Recognized incumbent-primary contest uncertainty.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Point estimate too far from market on <3 independent sources.
lessons.lesson: Incumbent congressional primaries: anchor to the strong incumbent base rate unless >=3 independent sources show a real threat.
lessons.judge_verdict: Resolved NO; brier 0.314 vs market 0.235. Critic: under-sourced. Defender: incumbent-primary uncertainty genuine. Judge: thin sourcing drove the miss.

lessons.what_went_right: Direction correct (NO), small error.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Did not pull enough independent review-tracking sources for the RT threshold.
lessons.lesson: RT-score threshold markets: pull multiple review-aggregator snapshots near close; a single snapshot is thin.
lessons.judge_verdict: Resolved NO; brier 0.048 vs 0.006. RT-score market; under-sourced but small miss.

lessons.what_went_right: Correctly near-certain on a NO outcome.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Sourcing breadth still under the >=3 bar per the rubric.
lessons.lesson: Even on near-certainties, log >=3 independent sources so the rubric and audit trail hold.
lessons.judge_verdict: Resolved NO; brier 0.010 vs 0.000. Well-calibrated near-certainty; critic flags sourcing only.

lessons.what_went_right: Called YES correctly (Trump made 0 June Mar-a-Lago trips); Brier 0.0064 ~ market.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Rationale did not name 3 independent sources or an explicit CI.
lessons.lesson: Even on high-confidence near-certain markets, write the base rate, an explicit confidence interval, and 3 named independent sources into the rationale so the reasoning is auditable — accuracy was fine, documentation was thin.
lessons.judge_verdict: Forecast was accurate (Brier<0.01, outcome matched). Critic's rubric misses are documentation gaps in the rationale_summary, not analytical errors; no change to probability warranted, but adopt explicit base-rate + CI + named-source hygiene.
lessons.disagreement: Critic graded the written reasoning (process rubric) as failing while the outcome was near-perfectly forecast — the gap is process-documentation vs. realized accuracy.

lessons.what_went_right: Called NO correctly; Brier 0.0100.
lessons.what_went_wrong: No base rate/CI/sources documented in reasoning.
lessons.lesson: Even on high-confidence near-certain markets, write the base rate, an explicit confidence interval, and 3 named independent sources into the rationale so the reasoning is auditable — accuracy was fine, documentation was thin.
lessons.judge_verdict: Forecast was accurate (Brier<0.01, outcome matched). Critic's rubric misses are documentation gaps in the rationale_summary, not analytical errors; no change to probability warranted, but adopt explicit base-rate + CI + named-source hygiene.
lessons.disagreement: Critic graded the written reasoning (process rubric) as failing while the outcome was near-perfectly forecast — the gap is process-documentation vs. realized accuracy.

lessons.what_went_right: Called NO correctly; Brier 0.0004 (beat model avg).
lessons.what_went_wrong: No explicit base rate/CI/named sources.
lessons.lesson: Even on high-confidence near-certain markets, write the base rate, an explicit confidence interval, and 3 named independent sources into the rationale so the reasoning is auditable — accuracy was fine, documentation was thin.
lessons.judge_verdict: Forecast was accurate (Brier<0.01, outcome matched). Critic's rubric misses are documentation gaps in the rationale_summary, not analytical errors; no change to probability warranted, but adopt explicit base-rate + CI + named-source hygiene.
lessons.disagreement: Critic graded the written reasoning (process rubric) as failing while the outcome was near-perfectly forecast — the gap is process-documentation vs. realized accuracy.

lessons.what_went_right: Called NO correctly (no House visit to Iran); Brier 0.0002.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Base rate for diplomatic visits not stated; no CI.
lessons.lesson: Even on high-confidence near-certain markets, write the base rate, an explicit confidence interval, and 3 named independent sources into the rationale so the reasoning is auditable — accuracy was fine, documentation was thin.
lessons.judge_verdict: Forecast was accurate (Brier<0.01, outcome matched). Critic's rubric misses are documentation gaps in the rationale_summary, not analytical errors; no change to probability warranted, but adopt explicit base-rate + CI + named-source hygiene.
lessons.disagreement: Critic graded the written reasoning (process rubric) as failing while the outcome was near-perfectly forecast — the gap is process-documentation vs. realized accuracy.

lessons.what_went_right: Called NO correctly (Vance no Iran visit); Brier 0.0004.
lessons.what_went_wrong: No 3 named sources or CI.
lessons.lesson: Even on high-confidence near-certain markets, write the base rate, an explicit confidence interval, and 3 named independent sources into the rationale so the reasoning is auditable — accuracy was fine, documentation was thin.
lessons.judge_verdict: Forecast was accurate (Brier<0.01, outcome matched). Critic's rubric misses are documentation gaps in the rationale_summary, not analytical errors; no change to probability warranted, but adopt explicit base-rate + CI + named-source hygiene.
lessons.disagreement: Critic graded the written reasoning (process rubric) as failing while the outcome was near-perfectly forecast — the gap is process-documentation vs. realized accuracy.

lessons.what_went_right: Called NO correctly (Rubio no Iran visit); Brier 0.0001.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Base rate/sources/CI not documented.
lessons.lesson: Even on high-confidence near-certain markets, write the base rate, an explicit confidence interval, and 3 named independent sources into the rationale so the reasoning is auditable — accuracy was fine, documentation was thin.
lessons.judge_verdict: Forecast was accurate (Brier<0.01, outcome matched). Critic's rubric misses are documentation gaps in the rationale_summary, not analytical errors; no change to probability warranted, but adopt explicit base-rate + CI + named-source hygiene.
lessons.disagreement: Critic graded the written reasoning (process rubric) as failing while the outcome was near-perfectly forecast — the gap is process-documentation vs. realized accuracy.

lessons.what_went_right: Correctly priced a near-zero-probability tail threshold; beat market Brier.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Rationale summary omitted an explicit base rate and confidence interval.
lessons.lesson: Even on easy directional threshold calls, state the base rate and a rough confidence interval in the rationale so the record is auditable.
lessons.judge_verdict: Forecast was correct and beat the market on Brier (0.010 vs 0.018); low prob on a +0.1% MoM CPI threshold that did not hit was well-founded given the observed disinflation trend. Critic's flags (no explicit CI, base rate not restated) are process-cosmetic here, not causal to any error.
lessons.disagreement: Critic penalized missing confidence interval; Defender showed the point estimate was correct and market-beating, so the gap is presentation not accuracy.

lessons.what_went_right: Established a base rate for MoM CPI distribution.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Diverged from market price without ≥3 independent sources backing the divergence; lost to market on Brier.
lessons.lesson: On tight CPI/threshold buckets near the distribution mode, do not diverge from market price unless ≥3 independent sources justify it; otherwise anchor closer to the market.
lessons.judge_verdict: Outcome NO; forecast was directionally acceptable but slightly worse than market on Brier (0.130 vs 0.093). Critic correctly notes the divergence from market-implied (0.115) was not justified with independent sourcing — on a threshold this close to the mode, deferring toward the market price would have scored better.
lessons.disagreement: Critic flagged unjustified market divergence; Defender concedes the divergence was unearned here (thin sourcing), so critic wins this rubric line.

lessons.what_went_right: Tracked the market's read of day-to-day reporting; no position was taken so no P&L damage.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Assigned literally zero probability to an event with a nonzero base rate, then resolved YES (Brier ~1.0). Undercut even the market's 0.55%. No base rate, no CI, no divergence justification — critic correct on all four rubric fails.
lessons.lesson: Never emit 0.0 on a multi-week human-decision window. A first-time candidate dropping out over ~6 months has a base rate of several percent regardless of current reporting; floor tails at the base rate and never price below the market on a tail without documented cause.
lessons.judge_verdict: CRITIC UPHELD. The miss itself was near-unforeseeable (market said 0.55%), but the 0.0 forecast is a process failure independent of outcome: never-say-zero is a precommitted rule, not hindsight.
lessons.disagreement: Defender argued the event was a shock the market also missed; Judge accepted that for the MISS but not for the ZERO.

lessons.what_went_right: Direction correct and large edge vs market (0.0008 vs 0.035, resolved NO); the strike fade thesis held.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Near-zero tail (0.0008) without a confidence interval or divergence justification; the win does not validate the extremity.
lessons.lesson: Fading an over-priced CPI strike is the right trade, but keep the tail floor: monthly CPI prints have surprised by 0.1pp+ often enough that sub-1% forecasts on an adjacent strike are miscalibrated even when they win.
lessons.judge_verdict: PARTIAL. Outcome vindicated the fade; critic upheld on process (unjustified extremity). Keep the fade, raise the floor.

lessons.what_went_right: Beat the market (0.098 vs 0.155, resolved NO) with divergence explicitly justified — critic passed that rubric item.
lessons.what_went_wrong: No confidence interval; source independence unclear.
lessons.lesson: The YoY strike fade with an acknowledged data gap was well-reasoned; keep documenting why the market's higher tail is unjustified — that justification is what separated this from the T0.0 critique.
lessons.judge_verdict: DEFENDER LARGELY UPHELD. Process was adequate; add CI discipline.

lessons.what_went_right: Won vs market (0.0002 vs 0.015, resolved NO).
lessons.what_went_wrong: Effectively zero tail again; no independent-source evidence cited (critic's biggest miss: three_independent_sources).
lessons.lesson: Same family as T3.9 but forecast 0.0002 — the zero-tail habit repeats across CPI strikes. Set a standing floor (>=0.005) for any single-print macro strike regardless of distance.
lessons.judge_verdict: CRITIC UPHELD on process. Two zero-tail flags in one resolution batch = systematic, not incidental.

lessons.what_went_right: Correct NO read (resolved 0); divergence justified via suppression mechanism — rubric pass.
lessons.what_went_wrong: No explicit base rate for recovery speed; no CI.
lessons.lesson: Crisis-suppressed logistics series revert slowly; citing the suppression mechanism justified the divergence and the critic passed it. Continue anchoring maritime-flow markets on the mechanism, but state the recovery base rate explicitly.
lessons.judge_verdict: DEFENDER UPHELD with base-rate documentation caveat.

lessons.lesson: WIN: locked entry p~0.95 beat market (Brier 0.0025 vs 0.0121); Trump attended an event he publicly committed to — high base rate, correct direction. Critic's low-base-rate 'miss' rejected as directionally confused (Defender). Upheld process note: even on confident forecasts near the market price, log an explicit CI and cite >=3 independent sources with a one-line divergence justification.

lessons.what_went_right: Forecaster correctly stayed out: my_prob=1.0, lean=NONE (no +EV at the ask, modal YES). The LLM did not lose this — it read the near-certain YES correctly and declined the trade.
lessons.what_went_wrong: Scored loss (brier 0.4356 vs market 0.3306) came from a money-path/atlas NO-fade committed at entry_probability~0.34 (reconcile scores pos.entry_probability, not the forecast). The favorite-longshot screen faded a Rotten Tomatoes score-threshold contract on a critically-acclaimed prestige film (Nolan Odyssey, ~98% RT), which resolved YES.
lessons.lesson: Do not let the atlas/screen fade RT/culture 'score >= threshold' contracts on prestige/critically-acclaimed films where the near-certain YES is real. The favorite-longshot prior does not apply when YES is genuinely ~99% (concentrated critical consensus). Carve-out: suppress NO-fades on RT-score threshold markets when yes_ask >= ~0.95 with corroborating source consensus. Structural/basket error, not a forecaster error.

rubric_scores.note: critic scored forecaster rationale; realized loss attributable to atlas NO-fade, not the forecaster

lessons.judge_verdict: UPHELD-REDIRECTED: critic scored the forecaster p=1.0 rationale (biggest_miss=confidence_interval), a red herring since the forecaster stayed out. Actionable defect is the atlas NO-fade selection; lesson redirected to the screen.
lessons.disagreement: Critic faulted forecaster for no CI on a near-certain call; Defender showed forecaster took no position, so CI critique is immaterial to the realized loss, which is entirely a money-path fade.
